message.content:
I agree we remove --detach/--delay, but have both repo's committed by the first invocation is "better", IMO.
Why, because the session information that occurred up to issuing the `vc-x1 push ..` but not including it
will be preserved on a successful completion. You're idea will save the code repo command but it still won't
have the bot repo command. If the user want's that they can manually execute `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude`
and the user will then achieve their goal, both repo's @'s will be empty.

Actually a shell script with something like:
```
vc-x1 push ...` && vc-x1 finalize --squash --repo .claude`
```

Could do it, I think, thought?

content.text:
Agreed — push doing both repos inline as the single, self-contained default is the better call. My two-command variant only buys one extra command in the record at the cost of depending on bot discipline every cycle; not worth it. Conceded. And you're right that the manual-finalize path genuinely achieves the empty-@ goal, for a reason worth stating explicitly: **the self-reference trap only binds the bot** — a human running `vc-x1 finalize` from their own terminal generates no session data, so after the bot's turn ends, the tail on disk is complete and a squash truly empties @. The 10s delay was an attempt to approximate "after the turn ends" from inside the turn; the human just *is* after the turn.

On the script — mechanically it runs, but three caveats, one of which I think changes the shape:

**1. Run as one bot command, the `&&` one-liner folds in almost nothing.** The tail you want to capture (the push command's result, the end-of-turn transcript writes) is written to `.claude` *after* the shell line exits — while the script runs, the bot is blocked and the harness is quiet. So the finalize would squash a near-empty @ and the real tail still lands after. The tidy-up only has teeth when it runs after quiescence: the human in a terminal, or at minimum the bot in a *later, separate* command. As a bot-executed `&&` chain it's a no-op with side effects.

**2. It needs `--push main`, or local and remote diverge.** Squashing @ into the already-pushed session commit rewrites it (new SHA, same change-id); local `main` follows the rewrite, `main@origin` still has the old SHA. Without a push, the next cycle's `jj git push` silently becomes a forced update — surprising at a distance.

**3. Which surfaces the real design question: the post-push squash is a history rewrite of a published commit, every time.** It's why finalize carries `--ignore-immutable`. Change-ids survive so ochid links are fine, but the GitHub mirror's top commit gets replaced, and any other clone of the session repo diverges — notably, the fc ochid recovery in Bugs #2 depended on a backup-workspace clone, exactly the thing repeated force-pushes degrade. An alternative for the tidy-up: instead of squashing into the pushed commit, **commit the tail as a new small commit** and push — append-only remote, no `--ignore-immutable`, no forced updates, and the goal (both @'s empty) is equally achieved. Cost: an extra tail commit in the journal for cycles where the user opts in. That's the trade: fold-in keeps one-commit-per-cycle history but rewrites published commits; append keeps the journal append-only but adds a commit. I'd lean append, but it's a taste call and it's your journal.

So the converged design as I see it:

- **push**: all stages inline — commit both, bookmarks, push app, then an inline squash of the (tiny) micro-tail into the session commit and `jj git push --bookmark main -R .claude`. No detach, no delay, no child. If the session push fails, push fails visibly, right there.
- **Preflight backstop**: error/auto-push when `.claude main` is ahead of `main@origin` — cheap insurance against this whole failure class.
- **Optional human tidy-up** after the bot goes quiet: `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude` in whichever flavor you pick (squash+force-push vs. append-commit+push) for true empty-@.
- `--detach`/`--delay` retired from the push flow (deprecate or keep on finalize standalone, separately decidable).

Want me to write this up — update Bugs #1 with the diagnosis and add a Todo entry with this design sketch — so it's ready to pick up as a cycle?

message.content:
Got if the bot executes a script with both commands it'll still way until the entire script
finishes and as you say the finalize in the script captures nearly nothing from the session.
So, if the bot executed `vc-x1 push ..` both repos will be pushed and we'll have a "full"
session info minus any of the push command.
Now, after `vc-x1 push ..` completes, if the bot executes
`vc-x1 finalize --ignore-immutable --squash --repo .claude` itself the `vc-x1 push ..`
will be present but @ won't be empty.

Possible solution; we add a `finalize-bot` sub-command which is `vc-x1 finalize --ignore-immutable --squash --repo .claude`.
The we recommend the user execute `vc-x1 finalize-bot`, themselves if they want @ to be empty on the bot repo
and the bot always just executes `vc-x1 push ..` as it does now.

Thoughts?

content.text:
That division of labor is clean and I'd go with it: **the bot's protocol never changes — it runs `vc-x1 push` and nothing else** — and the empty-@ refinement is a strictly optional, human-invoked command that exists because only someone *outside* the session can capture the whole tail. Each party does the thing only it can do. It also degrades gracefully: nobody runs `finalize-bot`, and the tail simply rides into the next cycle's session commit; run it sometimes, and those cycles' records are complete. No discipline anywhere in the loop.

A few details to pin down on `finalize-bot`:

- **It should push too.** Squash-without-push leaves local `main` on the rewritten commit while `main@origin` holds the old SHA, and the *next* cycle's push then does a surprise forced update. Better that `finalize-bot` = squash + push main, so the forced update happens right there, deliberately, in the human's terminal where its output is visible. (The force-push per use is inherent to the squash flavor — as an explicit human action rather than an every-cycle bot side effect, that's a much smaller blast radius.)
- **No flags needed at all.** `--ignore-immutable` is already hardcoded inside finalize's squash (`src/finalize.rs:449`), and the session repo is pinned to `main`, so `finalize-bot` can be a zero-argument subcommand: resolve `.claude` from the workspace root, squash `@ → @-`, push `main`, delay 0, no detach. Internally it's just a preset over the existing `FinalizeParams` — a thin subcommand, no new machinery. The Bugs #2 ochid-drop guard already protects the squash path for free.
- **Make it safe to run anytime**: if @ is already empty, print "nothing to fold" and exit 0, so the user never has to check first.
- Bonus: the bot *may* also run it as a separate later command when a cycle's record matters — that captures the push command itself (leaving only finalize-bot's own record), same command, no special casing.

So the full fix package for Bugs #1:

1. `push` runs everything inline — replace the `finalize-claude` shell-out/detach/delay with an inline squash of the micro-tail plus `jj git push --bookmark main -R .claude`. Session-push failures become visible push failures.
2. Preflight backstop: error (or auto-push) when `.claude main` is ahead of `main@origin`.
3. New `finalize-bot` subcommand as above; recommend it to the human in docs/cycle-protocol as the optional empty-@ step.
4. Retire `--detach`/`--delay` from the push flow.

Want me to write this up now — Bugs #1 gets the diagnosis, and a Todo entry gets this design — so it's ready to pick up as a cycle?

file.content:
# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](/AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. **vc-x1 push: pause point between commit and publish
   stages.** The merge non-ff close-out is a three-step
   sequence:
   - commit the close-out pair locally (normal 1:1 commit
     stages, ochids injected both directions)
   - rebase the code-side close-out into the merge (chids
     survive rebase, so every ochid stays valid)
   - publish

   Push has no supported stop after `commit-claude`, so today
   the recipe pre-commits both sides manually and resumes via
   `--from bookmark-set --yes` — skipping exactly the stages
   that inject `ochid:` trailers.
   - Add a stop after the commit stages (`--to commit-claude`
     or `--no-publish`; name open); the existing `--from
     bookmark-set` is already the resume half.
   - Retires the close-out workaround; the merge commit
     carries its code→bot ochid because it was injected
     normally, before the rebase.
   - Together with the Todo "push/sync: bookmark is
     code-repo-only; pin the bot repo to main", completes
     the trapezoidal-commit workflow (1:1 bot↔code
     throughout; the merge is a code-side-only shape
     operation).

130	     (`cycle-protocol.md`, design notes) carry ordinary
131	     numbered lists that aren't managed sequences — a walk
132	     would false-positive (markdown renders `1. 1. 1.` as
133	     1-2-3, a legitimate prose pattern).
134	   - Override args follow the `--init-from` convention:
135	     positional files/dirs (a dir → its `*.md`) plus an
136	     `@<file>` manifest, additive — for ad-hoc validation of
137	     a specific file.

file.content:
operation).

2. **Version-number protocol is fragile — versions are
   baked into titles/bodies/todo/done/chores before the
   change lands.** The cycle protocol embeds an `X.Y.Z-N`
   version in commit titles and bodies, `## Todo` /
   `## Done` entries, and chores headers — all written
   while the work is in progress, i.e. before it lands.
   But version numbers are subject to change: in a public,
   merge-based flow (e.g. Linux), the version a change
   ships under is only fixed when it merges into `main`,
   so the landing version can't be anticipated while the
   work is underway. Pervasive version-in-text is
   therefore fragile for any non-linear / multi-contributor
   workflow. Promoted from Ideas at 0.65.2-0; slated for
   the cycle after 0.65.2.
   - Open question: what identifies a cycle's commits if
     not a pre-assigned version?
     - Needs to be unique within some agreed upon domain.
       A contributors email address would do it, but also
       a UUID (short-version) for a contribution. I could
       imagine a UUID generated from the initial email/issue
       that and then "version number" schema appended to that.
   - Surfaces to update once the identifier is chosen:
     cycle-protocol.md (title shape, Numbering), AGENTS.md
     (commit-recording headers), and the `vc-x1` validators
     that parse `(X.Y.Z)` strings.
3. **sync follow-up: extract `move-bookmark` command.** The
   "put the bookmark / `@` where it belongs" step at the end
   of sync (reposition logic) is useful standalone — e.g. the
   t1B scenario where `main` is right but `@` isn't on it —
   and deserves an honestly-named command instead of a mode.
   - `vc-x1 move-bookmark` (name open): no fetch; move `@`
     (and optionally the bookmark) onto a target under the
     same safety rules as sync's reposition step.
   - Sync's final step becomes a call to the same logic.
   - Follow-up to the 0.67.0 single-mode sync cycle.
4. **sync follow-up: push preflight in-process; drop
   `--check`; revisit push auto-rollback.** Push's preflight
   shells out to `vc-x1 sync --check` — a verify-only pass
   that is both racy (remote can move before the user's
   later apply) and not actually read-only (jj's fetch
   auto-ffs tracked bookmarks). Follow-up to the 0.67.0
   single-mode sync cycle.
   - Preflight becomes a real in-process sync (stop-on-error
     halts the push before anything is committed); the
     `sync --check` shell-out and PATH dependency go away.
   - Remove the deprecated hidden `--check` alias once
     nothing invokes it.
   - Apply the stop-on-error + `vc-x1 revert` philosophy to
     push's commit-stage rollback (today it auto-runs
     `jj op restore`, hiding the evidence).
5. **validate-numbering: rename the pair, check all
   sequence-managed notes files generically.** `validate-todo`
   / `fix-todo` only operate on the single file passed, so a
   renumber slip in `bugs.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, or
   `todo.md`'s `## Ideas` section passes unnoticed — too weak
   for a pre-commit gate. Prereq for the pre-commit doc
   validators (Todo "pre-commit: single rule ...").
   - Rename the pair: `validate-todo` → `validate-numbering`,
     `fix-todo` → `fix-numbering` — they validate numbered-
     sequence integrity, not todos specifically.
   - Generic detection: for every `#…#` section, validate the
     column-0 `^\d+\.␠` entries form a contiguous 1..N run.
     Drops the Todo/Bugs special-casing; auto-covers
     `## Ideas` and any new numbered section. Keep the
     column-0 anchor so indented sub-lists aren't counted.
   - Default scope: a fixed list of sequence-managed notes
     files (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`) so the
     no-arg pre-commit run covers them all. Fixed rather than
     a `notes/**.md` walk because prose docs
     (`cycle-protocol.md`, design notes) carry ordinary
     numbered lists that aren't managed sequences — a walk
     would false-positive (markdown renders `1. 1. 1.` as
     1-2-3, a legitimate prose pattern).
   - Override args follow the `--init-from` convention:
     positional files/dirs (a dir → its `*.md`) plus an
     `@<file>` manifest, additive — for ad-hoc validation of
     a specific file.
   - Open: revisit fixed-vs-glob at implementation if the

input.new_string:
1. **`vc-x1 push`: the session-repo push silently never happens
   when the bot runs push sandboxed.** Observed in
   [tprobe](https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe) 2026-07-14:
   both local repos fully consistent and the GitHub code repo
   current, but
   [tprobe.claude](https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe.claude)
   stuck 8 commits behind (`jj bookmark list --all-remotes -R
   .claude`: `main@origin (behind by 8 commits)`).
   - **Root cause (diagnosed 2026-07-14).** Push's
     `finalize-claude` stage doesn't push `.claude` itself — it
     spawns a detached (`setsid`) child, `vc-x1 finalize
     --squash --push main --delay 10 --detach`, which sleeps
     10s before squash / bookmark / push. A sandboxed Bash
     command kills every process in its pid namespace when the
     foreground command exits; `setsid` escapes the process
     group but not the namespace. Reproduced in this project's
     sandbox: a `setsid` child spawned from a sandboxed command
     was killed during its sleep, before doing any work. The
     bot thinks the same kill explains tprobe: its sandbox is
     always-on (`autoAllowBashIfSandboxed: true`,
     `allowUnsandboxedCommands: false`) with `vc-x1 push *`
     permission-allowed, so every push ran sandboxed.
   - **Evidence.** tprobe app-repo op log: 8 successful `push
     bookmark main to git remote origin` ops (the in-process
     `push-app` stage; network was fine). tprobe `.claude` op
     log: zero squash or push ops since the repo was cloned —
     the child never executed even its first command. tprobe's
     `.vc-x1/` state dir is empty — every push "completed all
     stages" from vc-x1's point of view.
   - **Why it's silent.** The child dies by SIGKILL, so no
     failure marker is written — and marker writes to
     `~/.cache/vc-x1` are outside the sandbox write allowlist
     anyway (`write_failure_marker` swallows errors by design).
     `verify_completion_sanity` checks local bookmark positions
     only — all correct. Related: `CliLogger::new` panics
     (`.expect`) when the `--log` path can't be opened, so a
     sandbox that denies `/tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log` kills the
     child at startup, before marker-writing — same silent
     symptom by another path.
   - **Side effect.** The trailing-session-data squash also
     never runs; the tail rides into the next cycle's session
     commit (misplaced, not lost).
   - **Fix direction:** see the Todo "push: inline the
     session-repo push" — inline squash+push in push itself,
     preflight backstop (`.claude main` ahead of `main@origin`
     is an error), `finalize` repurposed as the user-run
     empty-@ tidy-up, `--detach`/`--delay` retired.
   - **Recovery** for an affected project: `jj git push
     --bookmark main -R <proj>/.claude` from an unsandboxed
     shell.

2.

   wnwssolzozlp  /uynvlokxmrus          feat: seed no_std core + tp_pc parity example
   puspmmyotxww  /lppvkqzpsqmt          docs: no_std core phase split + unwrap lints
   xmrqyzymulzt  /xwlvxuxyorrr          chore: scaffold crate, open no_std core cycle
   xzzmntkqtryk  /vpxqvwtnvxok          Initial commit
   wink@3900x 26-07-14T18:46:12.274Z:~/data/prgs/rust/tprobe/.claude (main*+1)
   ```
   And on github at https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe/commits/main/ we see
   there are only 5 commits, the last being "feat: seed no_std core + tp_pc parity example".

   I believe this is a bug in vc-x1.

input.new_string:
1. **`vc-x1 push`: the session-repo push silently never happens
   when the bot runs push sandboxed.** Observed in
   [tprobe](https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe) 2026-07-14:
   both local repos fully consistent and the GitHub code repo
   current, but
   [tprobe.claude](https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe.claude)
   stuck 8 commits behind (`jj bookmark list --all-remotes -R
   .claude`: `main@origin (behind by 8 commits)`).
   - **Root cause (diagnosed 2026-07-14).** Push's
     `finalize-claude` stage doesn't push `.claude` itself — it
     spawns a detached (`setsid`) child, `vc-x1 finalize
     --squash --push main --delay 10 --detach`, which sleeps
     10s before squash / bookmark / push. A sandboxed Bash
     command kills every process in its pid namespace when the
     foreground command exits; `setsid` escapes the process
     group but not the namespace. Reproduced in this project's
     sandbox: a `setsid` child spawned from a sandboxed command
     was killed during its sleep, before doing any work. The
     bot thinks the same kill explains tprobe: its sandbox is
     always-on (`autoAllowBashIfSandboxed: true`,
     `allowUnsandboxedCommands: false`) with `vc-x1 push *`
     permission-allowed, so every push ran sandboxed.
   - **Evidence.** tprobe app-repo op log: 8 successful `push
     bookmark main to git remote origin` ops (the in-process
     `push-app` stage; network was fine). tprobe `.claude` op
     log: zero squash or push ops since the repo was cloned —
     the child never executed even its first command. tprobe's
     `.vc-x1/` state dir is empty — every push "completed all
     stages" from vc-x1's point of view.
   - **Why it's silent.** The child dies by SIGKILL, so no
     failure marker is written — and marker writes to
     `~/.cache/vc-x1` are outside the sandbox write allowlist
     anyway (`write_failure_marker` swallows errors by design).
     `verify_completion_sanity` checks local bookmark positions
     only — all correct. Related: `CliLogger::new` panics
     (`.expect`) when the `--log` path can't be opened, so a
     sandbox that denies `/tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log` kills the
     child at startup, before marker-writing — same silent
     symptom by another path.
   - **Side effect.** The trailing-session-data squash also
     never runs; the tail rides into the next cycle's session
     commit (misplaced, not lost).
   - **Fix direction:** see the Todo "push: inline the
     session-repo push" — inline squash+push in push itself,
     preflight backstop (`.claude main` ahead of `main@origin`
     is an error), `finalize` repurposed as the user-run
     empty-@ tidy-up, `--detach`/`--delay` retired.
   - **Recovery** for an affected project: `jj git push
     --bookmark main -R <proj>/.claude` from an unsandboxed
     shell.

2.

content.content:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form).
4	
5	## Cycles
6	
7	A cycle has three phases:
8	
9	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
10	  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
11	  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
12	  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
13	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
14	    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
15	    statement + plan ladder).
16	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
17	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
18	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
19	  needs; each runs through the
20	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
21	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
22	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
23	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
24	    `## Done`.
25	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
26	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
27	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
28	    changed.
29	
30	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
31	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
32	result must always be published at close-out. See
33	[Pushing](#pushing).
34	
35	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
36	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
37	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
38	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
39	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
40	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
41	for the local-ladder mechanics.
42	
43	## Chores sections
44	
45	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
46	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle:
47	
48	- One section per cycle is the default.
49	- Per-commit sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a
50	  deliberate alternative.
51	
52	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
53	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
54	with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
55	`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
56	content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).
57	
58	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
59	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
60	AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).
61	
62	## Preparation
63	
64	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
65	
66	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
67	  `Commits:` ref** — see
68	  [Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references).
69	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
70	- **Update `Cargo.lock`** to match — run `cargo build`
71	  (or `cargo check`) so the lockfile's `vc-x1` version
72	  tracks `Cargo.toml` in the same commit.
73	- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
74	  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
75	  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
76	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
77	  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
78	  - A **plan ladder**.
79	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
80	  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
81	  close-out title.
82	
83	## Work-N
84	
85	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
86	implement the change. As many as needed:
87	
88	- Each commit runs through the
89	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
90	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
91	  visibility).
92	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
93	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
94	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
95	  scope grows enough (see
96	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
97	
98	## Close-out
99	
100	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
101	only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
102	what happens post-squash:
103	
104	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
105	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
106	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
107	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
108	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
109	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
110	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
111	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
112	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
113	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
114	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
115	    back-reference.
116	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
117	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
118	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
119	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
120	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
121	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
122	
123	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
124	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
125	see [Pushing](#pushing).
126	
127	## Numbering
128	
129	The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase —
130	the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:
131	
132	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
133	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
134	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
135	
136	Disambiguation:
137	
138	- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
139	  Preparation.
140	- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
141	  identifier `0`).
142	
143	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
144	
145	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
146	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
147	- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
148	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
149	
150	Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
151	`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
152	`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.
153	
154	Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
155	reports the active phase at build time.
156	
157	## Per-commit flow
158	
159	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
160	through:
161	
162	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
163	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
164	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
165	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
166	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
167	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
168	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
169	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
170	   1. `cargo fmt`
171	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
172	   3. `cargo test`
173	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
174	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
175	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
176	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
177	   changes and we iterate until complete.
178	6. **Write the commit description** — see
179	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
180	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
181	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
182	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
183	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
184	   keeps the verb visible):
185	
186	   ```
187	   jj commit -m \
188	   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
189	   -m "<intro paragraph>
190	
191	   - file1: gist
192	   - file2: gist
193	
194	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
195	   -R .
196	   ```
197	
198	**Two overrides apply:**
199	
200	- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
201	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
202	  surface it; don't push through.
203	- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
204	  a review or question forward.
205	
206	## Commit description
207	
208	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)
209	with a version suffix:
210	
211	```
212	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
213	```
214	
215	### Title
216	
217	- ≤50 chars total (version suffix counts).
218	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
219	  `docs`, `chore`.
220	- Favor terse phrasings.
221	
222	### Body
223	
224	[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
225	wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:
226	
227	- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
228	  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
229	  files with multiple distinct changes:
230	
231	  ```
232	  - path/to/file1
233	    - first distinct change
234	    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
235	       with continuation indented 5
236	  ```
237	
238	  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
239	  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
240	  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
241	  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.
242	
243	- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
244	  in-session activity rather than code changes.
245	
246	### Trailer
247	
248	`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
249	[ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).
250	
251	## Reviewing changes
252	
253	Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
254	on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
255	editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:
256	
257	- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
258	- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
259	- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
260	- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev
261	
262	Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
263	it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
264	`jj show -r @-`.
265	
266	See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
267	close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
268	are in [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).
269	
270	## Pushing
271	
272	### Policy
273	
274	Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
275	progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
276	the cycle's result must be published.
277	
278	### Shape at close-out push
279	
280	At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
281	shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
282	the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
283	the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
284	needs approval), so choose deliberately.
285	
286	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
287	  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
288	  focused on one or two files.
289	- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
290	  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
291	  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
292	  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
293	  for the full setup sequence.
294	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
295	  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
296	  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
297	  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.
298	
299	Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
300	`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.
301	
302	### Merge non-ff recipe
303	
304	Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
305	close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
306	close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
307	(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
308	Work commit:
309	
310	1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
311	   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
312	   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
313	   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
314	   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.
315	
316	   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
317	   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
318	2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
319	   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
320	   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
321	   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
322	3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.
323	
324	**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
325	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
326	immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
327	push force-updates `main`.
328	The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
329	the close-out push.
330	
331	### vc-x1 push wrapper
332	
333	`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
334	`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.
335	
336	`vc-x1 push` injects the `ochid:` trailers itself (the
337	`commit-app` / `commit-claude` stages append them) — don't
338	hand-write them into the commit body or `--title`/`--body`.
339	
340	**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
341	[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
342	shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
343	planned:
344	
345	- Merge non-ff close-out without the manual pre-commits
346	  (`## Todo` entry "vc-x1 push: pause point between commit
347	  and publish stages") — commit stages run normally, pause
348	  for the merge rebase, resume via `--from bookmark-set`.
349	- N:1 code↔bot for code worked outside vc-x1 (`## Todo`
350	  entry "vc-x1 push: record uncovered code commits").
351	- Symmetric squash — demoted to `todo-backlog.md`
352	  ("vc-x1 push --squash"): after-publication squash is
353	  off the routine path now that Merge non-ff is the
354	  routine shape.
355	
356	Landed: per-repo bookmark names (0.68.0) — `<bookmark>` is
357	code-repo-only; the session repo is pinned to `main`
358	throughout push and sync.
359	
360	### .claude cadence
361	
362	**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
363	with every code commit in that push.
364	
365	The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
366	across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
367	snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
368	code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.
369	
370	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
371	on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
372	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
373	app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
374	pushes to the wrong remote ref.
375	
376	Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
377	session information created, which we don't worry about.
378	The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
379	remain empty — this behavior was verified in 0.64.0.
380	
381	### Bot communication at the reviews
382	
383	Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
384	"Checkpoint N", etc.):
385	
386	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
387	  "Work complete. Please review."
388	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
389	  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
390	  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
391	  --body ...` invocation by default.
392	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
393	  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
394	  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
395	  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.
396	
397	### After push or finalize: stop and wait
398	
399	After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
400	**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
401	.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
402	hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
403	proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
404	user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
405	step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
406	stop for the whole turn.
407	
408	`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
409	then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
410	no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
411	closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit anything
412	after the tool returns.
413	
414	Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
415	squashes → pushes):
416	
417	- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
418	  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
419	  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
420	  the invoke — into the snapshot.
421	- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
422	  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
423	  the bot to do more work.
424	- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
425	  after the tool returns.
426	
427	**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
428	finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
429	saying, say it *before* the invoke.
430	
431	### Recovery
432	
433	- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
434	  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
435	  finalize by hand:
436	
437	  ```
438	  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
439	  ```
440	
441	- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
442	  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
443	  extra activity by the bot's agents).
444	  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
445	    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
446	    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
447	    *after* the snapshot.
448	  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
449	    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
450	    change; it is not under the user's control.)
451	  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
452	    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
453	    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
454	    - Verified 0.64.0: two finalizes were executed and
455	      we purposely used the bot again and the second finalize
456	      captured everything.
457	  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
458	    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
459	    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
460	    and land after the snapshot. The remedy is the same: just
461	    run finalize again. This is why a single pass is never
462	    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
463	- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
464	  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
465	  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
466	  stale stage.
467	- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
468	  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
469	  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
470	  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
471	  push.
472	
473	## ochid trailers
474	
475	A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier
476	that survives rebases and describes (see
477	[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md)). An **ochid** trailer
478	on a commit body links it to its counterpart in the
479	other repo via that counterpart's chid, written as a
480	workspace-root-relative path. See
481	[`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) for the
482	multi-line trailer design (multi-user / forking).
483	
484	Path semantics — paths start with `/`, the workspace root
485	(the app repo); `/.claude` is the bot session sub-repo:
486	
487	- `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **app repo**.
488	- `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` references a change in the
489	  **`.claude` repo**.
490	
491	Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the
492	end of the commit body, using the chid's **12-character**
493	prefix:
494	
495	```
496	ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqowktp   # points to a .claude change
497	ochid: /wtpmottvxqzl           # points to an app change
498	```
499	
500	How many, and which direction:
501	
502	- **Code-side commits** each carry one
503	  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
504	  change ID.
505	- **The `.claude` commit** has one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
506	  per code commit in that push. More than one occurs on
507	  Merge non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in
508	  the cycle).
509	
510	Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change
511	IDs (first line app, second `.claude`).
512	
513	### Resolvability
514	
515	A change ID travels with its commit: a **pushed** commit
516	resolves to the same chid in every clone — cloning the
517	`.claude` repo gave the published `main` tip the same chid
518	as an existing clone. The bot thinks jj carries the change
519	ID in the git commit object, so it survives
520	`jj git clone` / fetch.
521	
522	The local-only case is the **working-copy `@`**: jj mints a
523	fresh random chid for `@` in each clone, so an unpushed `@`
524	is never a stable ochid target. This is why a `.claude`
525	ochid names `@-` (the last committed change), not `@`.
526	
527	### .vc-config.toml
528	
529	Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its
530	location within the workspace, so tools resolve these paths
531	without repeating the workspace path in every trailer:
532	
533	```toml
534	# In vc-x1 (workspace root):
535	[workspace]
536	path = "/"
537	
538	# In .claude (sub-repo):
539	[workspace]
540	path = "/.claude"
541	```
542	
543	## Iterative work
544	
545	When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
546	from incremental review, loop:
547	
548	1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
549	2. Make the next round of changes.
550	3. User reviews the round (see
551	   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
552	4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
553	   new empty `@`.
554	5. If not done, go to step 2.
555	
556	Same jj mechanics as a
557	[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
558	single-commit scope — the cycle's version suffix
559	doesn't change.
560	
561	## Sub-cycle ladders
562	
563	When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
564	[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
565	commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
566	**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
567	parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
568	for review and bisection only.
569	
570	### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder
571	
572	For each Work commit in the ladder:
573	
574	1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
575	2. Do the commit's work.
576	3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable** — build
577	   and clippy alone miss regressions until a later
578	   commit runs the full suite, raising bisection cost.
579	4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
580	   only (no version suffix); the sub-cycle Close-out
581	   collects everything into one final commit with the
582	   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker.
583	
584	### Navigating the ladder
585	
586	Common moves:
587	
588	- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
589	  from its base.
590	- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
591	  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
592	- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
593	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
594	  isolation.
595	
596	Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
597	descendants auto-rebase.
598	
599	### Close-out: squash the ladder
600	
601	When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:
602	
603	```
604	jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
605	```
606	
607	`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
608	keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
609	After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
610	intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.
611	
612	Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.
613	
614	For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
615	when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.
616	
617	### Recovery
618	
619	If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
620	prior commits:
621	
622	- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
623	  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
624	  parent.
625	- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
626	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
627	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
628	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
629	  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
630	  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
631	  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.
632	
633	# References
634	
635	- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
636	  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
637	- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) — multi-ochid
638	  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
639	- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
640	  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
641	  for squash-recipe experiments.
642	- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-commit ladder usage. The
643	  per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
644	  after a regression introduced in an early commit wasn't
645	  caught until a later one ran the full suite.
646

file.content:
# Cycle protocol

This protocol uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form).

## Cycles

A cycle has three phases:

- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically the top-ranked,
    #1) into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
    statement + plan ladder).
  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
  needs; each runs through the
  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
    `## Done`.
  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
    [chores section](#chores-sections).
  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
    changed.

A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
result must always be published at close-out. See
[Pushing](#pushing).

**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
for the local-ladder mechanics.

## Chores sections

A **chores section** is a `##` section in
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle:

- One section per cycle is the default.
- Per-commit sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a
  deliberate alternative.

The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).

Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live in
AGENTS.md [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions).

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
  `Commits:` ref** — see
  [Chores commit references](../AGENTS.md#chores-commit-references).
- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
- **Update `Cargo.lock`** to match — run `cargo build`
  (or `cargo check`) so the lockfile's `vc-x1` version
  tracks `Cargo.toml` in the same commit.
- **Move a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one) into
  `## In Progress` and the todo item should have:
  - A **bold title line** — that will be the chores
    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
  - A **succinct problem statement**; add if one is needed
  - A **plan ladder**.
- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
  close-out title.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
implement the change. As many as needed:

- Each commit runs through the
  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
  visibility).
- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
  [Pushing](#pushing)).
- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
  scope grows enough (see
  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
what happens post-squash:

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
  placeholder opened during Preparation):
  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
    chores `##` header already carries that string).
  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
    back-reference.
  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
  - Replace `## In Progress` with
    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
see [Pushing](#pushing).

## Numbering

The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase —
the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

Disambiguation:

- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
  Preparation.
- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
  identifier `0`).

**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:

- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle

Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.

Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
reports the active phase at build time.

## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
   for the loop-and-squash technique).
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
   cycle mandatory at close-out):
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   changes and we iterate until complete.
6. **Write the commit description** — see
   [Commit description](#commit-description).
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

## Commit description

[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)
with a version suffix:

```
<type>: <short description> (<version>)
```

### Title

- ≤50 chars total (version suffix counts).
- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
  `docs`, `chore`.
- Favor terse phrasings.

### Body

[Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:

- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
  files with multiple distinct changes:

  ```
  - path/to/file1
    - first distinct change
    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
       with continuation indented 5
  ```

  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.

- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
  in-session activity rather than code changes.

### Trailer

`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
[ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).

## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
on the way to commit. The user opens diffs in their
editor (Zed, VSCode); jj commands are for terminal:

- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev

Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
are in [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).

## Pushing

### Policy

Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
the cycle's result must be published.

### Shape at close-out push

At close-out the cycle's *work* is done; its *published
shape* is the remaining choice, made at push time. Surface
the options and get user approval before pushing — once on
the target, changing shape is a remote rewrite (force-push,
needs approval), so choose deliberately.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
  Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is
  focused on one or two files.
- **Merge non-ff** *(current default)* — `main` gains a
  merge commit (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via
  two parents. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. See [Merge non-ff recipe](#merge-non-ff-recipe)
  for the full setup sequence.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### Merge non-ff recipe

Setting up a [Merge non-ff](#shape-at-close-out-push)
close-out is a fixed sequence. `<closeout>` is the cycle's
close-out commit, `<prev>` the previous cycle's close-out
(the current `main` tip), `<work-tip>` the cycle's last
Work commit:

1. **Rebase the close-out into a merge** — `jj rebase -r
   <closeout> --onto <prev> --onto <work-tip>`.
   - `-r <closeout>` keeps the `<closeout>` commit in place.
   - `--onto <prev>` becomes its first parent (trunk).
   - `--onto <work-tip>` becomes the second parent.

   Together these make `<closeout>` a merge of
   `<prev>` + `<work-tip>`, forming a trapezoidal commit.
2. **Use `jj new <merge>`** to add an empty `@` above the
   merge. The rebase left `@` *on* the now-content-bearing
   merge, which git/IDE diff views show as uncommitted;
   `jj new` restores the clean empty `@` on top.
3. **Push** — `jj git push --bookmark main -R .`.

**Post-hoc caveat.** If the cycle was already pushed
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) its commits are
immutable: the rebase needs `--ignore-immutable` and the
push force-updates `main`.
The standard sequence assumes the merge is set up *before*
the close-out push.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps per-push mechanics. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for current flags.

`vc-x1 push` injects the `ochid:` trailers itself (the
`commit-app` / `commit-claude` stages append them) — don't
hand-write them into the commit body or `--title`/`--body`.

**Current limitation**: only fully supports the
[Keep separate](#shape-at-close-out-push) shape; other
shapes need manual jj steps. Improvements tracked /
planned:

- Merge non-ff close-out without the manual pre-commits
  (`## Todo` entry "vc-x1 push: pause point between commit
  and publish stages") — commit stages run normally, pause
  for the merge rebase, resume via `--from bookmark-set`.
- N:1 code↔bot for code worked outside vc-x1 (`## Todo`
  entry "vc-x1 push: record uncovered code commits").
- Symmetric squash — demoted to `todo-backlog.md`
  ("vc-x1 push --squash"): after-publication squash is
  off the routine path now that Merge non-ff is the
  routine shape.

Landed: per-repo bookmark names (0.68.0) — `<bookmark>` is
code-repo-only; the session repo is pinned to `main`
throughout push and sync.

### .claude cadence

**Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired
with every code commit in that push.

The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data
across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across
snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so
code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
pushes to the wrong remote ref.

Ending a session: if the user runs `/exit` there will be
session information created, which we don't worry about.
The user can close the terminal instead and `@` will
remain empty — this behavior was verified in 0.64.0.

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit/push.
  Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push ... --title ...
  --body ...` invocation by default.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) and the push target;
  wait for the user's choice before any `jj squash` /
  `jj rebase` / `jj git push` invocation.

### After push or finalize: stop and wait

After you **push** (cross the remote boundary) or launch
**finalize** on the `.claude` repo (`vc-x1 finalize --repo
.claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach`) — by
hand or via the `vc-x1 push` wrapper — you **MUST NEVER**
proceed (next step, edit, tool call, text output) until the
user explicitly directs you to continue. **Even when the next
step seems obvious — wait.** Treat push-or-finalize as a hard
stop for the whole turn.

`vc-x1 push` performs both as its tail stages — `push-app`
then the detached `vc-x1 finalize` on `.claude` — so there is
no gap left to speak in once the push is invoked. Put **all**
closing words *before* invoking it; do not purposely emit anything
after the tool returns.

Why (`.claude` is the live journal finalize snapshots →
squashes → pushes):

- **`--detach`**: lets the tool return immediately so the
  harness can keep flushing the *trailing* session-data — the
  tool call's own transcript and any words emitted *before*
  the invoke — into the snapshot.
- **`--delay 10`**: gives the harness time to complete that
  flush before the snapshot is taken. It is **not** time for
  the bot to do more work.
- **silence**: the bot must not summarize or perform any work
  after the tool returns.

**Known slip**: the bot has emitted a summary after launching
finalize. There is no "harmless" closing line — if it's worth
saying, say it *before* the invoke.

### Recovery

- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
  finalize by hand:

  ```
  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
  ```

- **Run finalize again if `@` is non-empty** after the
  finalize's squash-and-commit (also desirable after
  extra activity by the bot's agents).
  - Why: finalize snapshots `.claude` at `--delay`, but the
    bot keeps writing session data while it runs — the tool's
    own invocation plus the bot's closing response land
    *after* the snapshot.
  - Safe to repeat: bot session data is append-only, so a
    re-run never conflicts or overwrites. (This could
    change; it is not under the user's control.)
  - Fix: run `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push
    <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach` another time to capture
    that tail; one extra pass is generally enough.
    - Verified 0.64.0: two finalizes were executed and
      we purposely used the bot again and the second finalize
      captured everything.
  - No guarantees: events outside the bot's control can leave
    `@` non-empty — e.g. the bot's back end may decide to
    squash/consolidate session data, which can take minutes
    and land after the snapshot. The remedy is the same: just
    run finalize again. This is why a single pass is never
    guaranteed to leave `@` empty.
- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
  stale stage.
- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
  (e.g. updating AGENTS.md or memory) requires `jj
  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
  push.

## ochid trailers

A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier
that survives rebases and describes (see
[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md)). An **ochid** trailer
on a commit body links it to its counterpart in the
other repo via that counterpart's chid, written as a
workspace-root-relative path. See
[`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) for the
multi-line trailer design (multi-user / forking).

Path semantics — paths start with `/`, the workspace root
(the app repo); `/.claude` is the bot session sub-repo:

- `ochid: /<chid>` references a change in the **app repo**.
- `ochid: /.claude/<chid>` references a change in the
  **`.claude` repo**.

Trailers are blank-line-separated `key: value` lines at the
end of the commit body, using the chid's **12-character**
prefix:

```
ochid: /.claude/xvzvruqowktp   # points to a .claude change
ochid: /wtpmottvxqzl           # points to an app change
```

How many, and which direction:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
  change ID.
- **The `.claude` commit** has one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
  per code commit in that push. More than one occurs on
  Merge non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in
  the cycle).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change
IDs (first line app, second `.claude`).

### Resolvability

A change ID travels with its commit: a **pushed** commit
resolves to the same chid in every clone — cloning the
`.claude` repo gave the published `main` tip the same chid
as an existing clone. The bot thinks jj carries the change
ID in the git commit object, so it survives
`jj git clone` / fetch.

The local-only case is the **working-copy `@`**: jj mints a
fresh random chid for `@` in each clone, so an unpushed `@`
is never a stable ochid target. This is why a `.claude`
ochid names `@-` (the last committed change), not `@`.

### .vc-config.toml

Each repo contains a `.vc-config.toml` that identifies its
location within the workspace, so tools resolve these paths
without repeating the workspace path in every trailer:

```toml
# In vc-x1 (workspace root):
[workspace]
path = "/"

# In .claude (sub-repo):
[workspace]
path = "/.claude"
```

## Iterative work

When work for a single commit (the **target**) benefits
from incremental review, loop:

1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target.
2. Make the next round of changes.
3. User reviews the round (see
   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
4. `jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a
   new empty `@`.
5. If not done, go to step 2.

Same jj mechanics as a
[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
single-commit scope — the cycle's version suffix
doesn't change.

## Sub-cycle ladders

When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
for review and bisection only.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable** — build
   and clippy alone miss regressions until a later
   commit runs the full suite, raising bisection cost.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
   only (no version suffix); the sub-cycle Close-out
   collects everything into one final commit with the
   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves:

- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
  from its base.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
  isolation.

Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.

For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.

# References

- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) — multi-ochid
  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.
- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-commit ladder usage. The
  per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
  after a regression introduced in an early commit wasn't
  caught until a later one ran the full suite.

input.description: Show 0.67.1 and 0.68.0 chores sections and refs

content.content:
Plain `vc-x1 sync` — the invocation a user reaches for — now
converges the bookmark *and* seats `@` on it in both repos, and
a failed sync ends with inspectable state plus an explicit,
scoped undo (`vc-x1 revert`). Verified on the real test-repo-1
clones: syncing t1B after a t1A push moved `main` and `@` for
the first time.

## docs: todo cleanup + trapezoid entries (0.67.1)

Commits: [[17]]

A session on the trapezoidal-commit workflow (work a code-repo
bookmark 1:1, land it as the merge non-ff close-out) turned
into a reshape of the push-related todos. Verified that
`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` applies the one bookmark name to both
repos — violating the bot-repo-linear-on-`main` invariant the
moment a feature bookmark is pushed. Single-commit cycle.

- New Todo #1 "push/sync: bookmark is code-repo-only; pin the
  bot repo to main" — the invariant fix; prereq for working
  the code repo on feature bookmarks.
- New Todo #2 "vc-x1 push: pause point between commit and
  publish stages" — the trapezoid close-out stays 1:1: commit
  stages run normally, pause for the merge rebase (chids
  survive rebase, so every ochid stays valid), resume via
  `--from bookmark-both`; retires the manual pre-commit
  workaround.
- "vc-x1 push: record uncovered code commits (N:1 code↔bot)"
  re-scoped to code worked outside vc-x1 (no bot pairings
  exist); the trapezoid close-out is explicitly out of scope.
- "vc-x1 push --squash" demoted to todo-backlog.md —
  after-publication squash is off the routine path now that
  merge non-ff is the routine shape; pre-publication squash
  needs no tooling.
- cycle-protocol.md push-wrapper improvements list synced to
  the reshaped todos.

### As-built ladder

- 0.67.1 single-commit cycle: todo reshape + backlog demotion
  + wrapper-list sync + this section.

## feat: pin bot repo to main (0.68.0)

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` applied the one bookmark name to both
repos (preflight tracking check, `bookmark-both`,
`finalize --push`), and sync's classify/fetch used the passed
bookmark for every repo — but the bot repo is a linear journal
on `main` by design. Pushing a feature bookmark would create
and push that bookmark in the bot repo, leave the bot `main`
behind, and wedge the next sync's `reposition_session`. Prereq
for the trapezoidal-commit workflow (branch the code repo; bot
stays on `main`).

Two adjacent sync warts surfaced (and were fixed) mid-cycle
while dogfooding: the unconditional `jj new main` on the
session repo, and the three-lines-to-say-nothing clean-case
output.

### As-built ladder

- 0.68.0-0 prep: backfill Commits:, bump version, pick up
  todo, open chores section
- 0.68.0-1 sync: session repo pins `main` — tracking
  preflight + classify/act use a per-repo bookmark; tests
- 0.68.0-2 sync: `reposition_session` no-ops when `@-` is
  already the `main` tip — previously it always `jj new
  main`ed (empty `@`: chid/op churn; non-empty `@`: live
  session writes stranded on a sibling head); tests
- 0.68.0-3 sync: quiet output — clean case prints one
  summary line (`UP_TO_DATE_MSG` const shared with main.rs's

toolUseResult.stdout:
Plain `vc-x1 sync` — the invocation a user reaches for — now
converges the bookmark *and* seats `@` on it in both repos, and
a failed sync ends with inspectable state plus an explicit,
scoped undo (`vc-x1 revert`). Verified on the real test-repo-1
clones: syncing t1B after a t1A push moved `main` and `@` for
the first time.

## docs: todo cleanup + trapezoid entries (0.67.1)

Commits: [[17]]

A session on the trapezoidal-commit workflow (work a code-repo
bookmark 1:1, land it as the merge non-ff close-out) turned
into a reshape of the push-related todos. Verified that
`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` applies the one bookmark name to both
repos — violating the bot-repo-linear-on-`main` invariant the
moment a feature bookmark is pushed. Single-commit cycle.

- New Todo #1 "push/sync: bookmark is code-repo-only; pin the
  bot repo to main" — the invariant fix; prereq for working
  the code repo on feature bookmarks.
- New Todo #2 "vc-x1 push: pause point between commit and
  publish stages" — the trapezoid close-out stays 1:1: commit
  stages run normally, pause for the merge rebase (chids
  survive rebase, so every ochid stays valid), resume via
  `--from bookmark-both`; retires the manual pre-commit
  workaround.
- "vc-x1 push: record uncovered code commits (N:1 code↔bot)"
  re-scoped to code worked outside vc-x1 (no bot pairings
  exist); the trapezoid close-out is explicitly out of scope.
- "vc-x1 push --squash" demoted to todo-backlog.md —
  after-publication squash is off the routine path now that
  merge non-ff is the routine shape; pre-publication squash
  needs no tooling.
- cycle-protocol.md push-wrapper improvements list synced to
  the reshaped todos.

### As-built ladder

- 0.67.1 single-commit cycle: todo reshape + backlog demotion
  + wrapper-list sync + this section.

## feat: pin bot repo to main (0.68.0)

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` applied the one bookmark name to both
repos (preflight tracking check, `bookmark-both`,
`finalize --push`), and sync's classify/fetch used the passed
bookmark for every repo — but the bot repo is a linear journal
on `main` by design. Pushing a feature bookmark would create
and push that bookmark in the bot repo, leave the bot `main`
behind, and wedge the next sync's `reposition_session`. Prereq
for the trapezoidal-commit workflow (branch the code repo; bot
stays on `main`).

Two adjacent sync warts surfaced (and were fixed) mid-cycle
while dogfooding: the unconditional `jj new main` on the
session repo, and the three-lines-to-say-nothing clean-case
output.

### As-built ladder

- 0.68.0-0 prep: backfill Commits:, bump version, pick up
  todo, open chores section
- 0.68.0-1 sync: session repo pins `main` — tracking
  preflight + classify/act use a per-repo bookmark; tests
- 0.68.0-2 sync: `reposition_session` no-ops when `@-` is
  already the `main` tip — previously it always `jj new
  main`ed (empty `@`: chid/op churn; non-empty `@`: live
  session writes stranded on a sibling head); tests
- 0.68.0-3 sync: quiet output — clean case prints one
  summary line (`UP_TO_DATE_MSG` const shared with main.rs's
